generatedBy: claude-fable-5, blind generator prompt per preregistration.md

problems.text: i maintain the ci pipeline for a 40-person engineering team. our main test suite takes 35 minutes and roughly one in five runs fails on a test unrelated to the change, mostly timing-sensitive integration tests against a shared staging database. engineers have started retrying builds by habit, which doubles queue times on busy afternoons, and a few have begun merging with failing checks when a release is urgent. we run on self-hosted runners with a fixed budget, so i cannot simply add machines, and the release process requires a green build within two hours of the cut.

i run a postgres 13 instance backing a b2b saas product with about 900 gb of data. one append-heavy events table accounts for two thirds of the size, and queries that aggregate over the last 90 days have crept from 200 ms to 8 seconds over the past year. autovacuum falls behind during business hours, and the nightly reporting job now overlaps with the morning traffic peak in europe. we are on a managed provider, downtime windows are limited to sunday nights, and the application's orm generates most of the queries, so i can't easily hand-tune each one.

i operate three kubernetes clusters on eks that are two minor versions behind, and the oldest one hits end of support in ten weeks. about 60 workloads run there, owned by eight teams, and a handful still use deprecated apis that were removed in the next version. several stateful services use local persistent volumes pinned to specific nodes, which makes node replacement during upgrades risky. a previous upgrade attempt caused a 40-minute outage when a misconfigured admission webhook blocked pod scheduling cluster-wide. i have no staging cluster that mirrors production traffic, and the workload owners have little spare capacity this quarter.

i inherited a php monolith that powers an e-commerce site doing about 2 million euros a month. deploys happen by rsyncing files to four servers behind a load balancer, there is no test suite, and the previous developer left no documentation. small changes regularly break checkout in ways we only discover from customer emails. the business wants weekly feature releases for a holiday campaign starting in eight weeks. the codebase mixes templating, business logic, and raw sql in the same files, and the only environment besides production is a copy on my laptop with stale data.

i look after a kafka cluster that feeds a fraud-scoring service. one consumer group falls behind during evening peaks, with lag climbing to 20 million messages before slowly draining overnight. scoring decisions arriving more than a minute late are useless to the checkout flow, so during the backlog window we effectively run unprotected. the consumers are cpu-bound on json deserialization, the hot topic already has 48 partitions, and the producing team cannot change the message format until next quarter. adding consumer instances stopped helping once we matched the partition count, and finance has flagged our broker spend twice this year.

i own observability for a platform team, and our datadog bill has tripled in 18 months to a number now scheduled for cfo review. most of the spend is custom metrics and log ingestion from about 200 microservices, many emitting debug-level logs in production that nobody reads. when i sampled a week of usage, fewer than 15 percent of our dashboards had been opened in the last quarter, yet teams resist deleting anything because incidents occasionally hinge on an obscure metric. i need to cut spend meaningfully without being the person blamed the next time an outage is hard to diagnose.

i am the de facto security engineer at a 30-person startup. database passwords, api keys, and signing certificates live in a mix of .env files, ci variables, a shared password vault, and a few hardcoded strings we keep finding in repo history. last month a contractor's laptop was stolen and we could not say with confidence which credentials needed rotating, so we rotated almost everything and broke staging for two days. services run across heroku, aws, and a legacy vps. engineers push back hard on anything that adds friction to local development, and soc 2 fieldwork starts in three months.

i manage infrastructure as code for a mid-size company using terraform across roughly 40 aws accounts. over the years, engineers have made console changes during incidents that never made it back into code, so plans now show dozens of diffs nobody trusts. applying anything risks reverting a hotfix someone depends on, and two state files have grown so large that a plan takes 25 minutes. one shared module pinned to an old provider version blocks upgrades everywhere downstream. my team is two people, change freezes are imposed every quarter-end, and auditors want evidence that production actually matches the repository.

i run the on-call rotation for a payments backend with six engineers. we page on about 30 alerts a week, but postmortem records show only one in ten pages over the last quarter required any action. the noisiest alerts are threshold checks on per-host cpu and disk that fire during routine batch jobs. two engineers have quietly muted their phones overnight, and another cited on-call load in their resignation letter. compliance requires that certain payment-flow failures page within five minutes, so i cannot simply raise thresholds across the board, and nobody can confidently say which alerts actually protect the regulated paths.

i build the backend for a multi-tenant analytics product where all 4,000 customers share one api fleet and a single clickhouse cluster. a handful of enterprise tenants run scheduled exports at the top of each hour that spike query latency for everyone, doubling our p99 during those windows. contracts with two large customers include latency slas we now violate weekly. tenant ids are threaded inconsistently through the request path, so today we cannot reliably attribute load, let alone limit it. sales keeps selling unlimited api access, and moving big tenants onto isolated clusters is not in this year's budget.

i lead design at a 40-person b2b saas company. our figma library has 312 components but engineers ship from a react package that covers about half of them. designers detach instances to get the variants they need, engineers rebuild those screens with whatever props exist, and production drifts further from the mocks every sprint. we just closed a six-figure contract partly on the promise of a polished ui, the customer's review flagged inconsistent focus states, and i have two months and no extra headcount. leadership measures the design team on feature velocity, not consistency.

i run an online furniture store doing about $2m a year, with 70% of traffic on phones. checkout is four pages on a heavily customized shopify theme from 2019. analytics show 61% of mobile users who reach the shipping step never get to payment. that step pulls freight quotes that take eight seconds to load, and the page looks frozen while they do. customers call asking whether their order went through, and my one support person spends half her day on it. i can't replatform before the holiday season, and the freight api's response time is contractual and won't change.

i'm the only designer at a fintech startup offering business checking accounts. regulation requires we collect an ein, formation documents, and the id of every owner with a 25% stake before an account opens. signup currently asks for everything in one 14-field form, and 68% of founders drop off there, mostly on mobile where they don't have documents handy. compliance won't allow any provisional account, marketing wants signup to feel as light as a consumer app, and sales keeps manually onboarding big prospects over zoom, which doesn't scale past the ten they manage a week.

i design the dispatch screen for a trucking logistics platform. dispatchers monitor 80 to 200 active loads on dual 24-inch monitors and insist every load stay visible without scrolling, so the current table uses 11px type across 18 columns. new dispatchers take three months to read it fluently, and our two biggest customers have aging workforces complaining about eye strain. when i prototyped a roomier layout, veteran users revolted because hiding any column slows their exception-spotting. meanwhile sales demos this same screen to small fleets who find it terrifying and buy a competitor instead.

i'm a contractor redesigning a county benefits portal people use to apply for housing and food assistance. an advocacy group filed a complaint citing 140 wcag failures, and we're under a court-supervised deadline nine months out. the portal is 60-plus screens of forms maintained by three vendors on two different stacks, with shared header code nobody owns. many users are on five-year-old android phones, prepaid data plans, or library computers with 15-minute session limits and screen readers. any wording change to a form must be re-approved by state legal, which takes about six weeks per batch.

i manage communications for a 30-year-old children's literacy nonprofit. our logo exists in at least nine versions across chapter websites, donated billboards, embroidered volunteer shirts, and a decade of printed workbooks still sitting in classrooms. a board member's agency donated a sleek rebrand, but our largest donors are retired teachers who associate the old crayon-style mark with us, and last spring's donor survey showed recognition of that mark drives year-end giving. chapters print locally from whatever files they have, we have no shared asset server, and my entire design budget for the year is $12,000.

i'm a product manager on a nine-year-old project management tool with 60,000 paying teams. every acquisition and enterprise deal added a top-level nav item, and we're now at 13, plus a settings area with nine tabs that support says drives a quarter of all tickets. nps verbatims keep saying 'bloated' and 'can't find anything,' yet usage logs show every nav item has at least one large account using it daily. the last time we moved a menu item, three enterprise customers escalated to their account reps within a week. churn among new self-serve signups is creeping up every quarter.

i'm a design lead at a stock-trading app where 78% of sessions happen in dark mode, which we shipped years ago by mechanically inverting our light palette. the reds and greens we use for gains and losses now fail contrast on the dark background, marketing illustrations have baked-in white backgrounds that glow like billboards, and the charting library's gridlines vanish entirely. a colorblind user's teardown hit the front page of a trading subreddit last week. the palette is hardcoded separately in ios, android, and web codebases, and each platform team owns its own theming layer.

i design a meditation app that just launched in germany and japan. german translations run 30 to 40 percent longer than english, so our card-based home screen truncates session titles like 'breathing exercise for anxiety' into uselessness, and japanese line breaks split words in ways our localizers call embarrassing. the brand identity is built on a hand-lettered english typeface with no kanji support, so japanese screens silently fall back to a system font and look like a different product. our japanese app store screenshots are still in english, the tokyo marketing partner is asking pointed questions, and the next release window is five weeks out.

i run growth at a developer-tools startup selling api monitoring. our pricing page shows three tiers priced per host, but customers think in requests per month. trial users consistently pick the cheapest tier, hit the host limit in week one, and churn instead of upgrading, telling exit surveys they felt tricked. finance models revenue per host and won't approve changing the unit before the next board cycle. competitors publish simple pricing calculators we can't copy without exposing how unfavorable our unit is at small scale. trial-to-paid conversion sits at 4% against an 11% industry benchmark.

i write a weekly newsletter about personal finance with about 12,000 subscribers. open rates have slid from 48 percent to 31 percent over the past year and i can't tell why. i have an archive of 300 past issues in the email platform, but the analytics only show totals per issue, not which sections people actually read. sponsors pay based on opens, and two have already asked for discounts. i write every issue solo on monday nights, so anything i change about my process has to fit in about four hours a week.

i produce a twice-weekly interview podcast that has been running four years, 410 episodes in the back catalog. none of the episodes have transcripts, so nothing is searchable, and guests keep emailing to ask where they said a particular thing. a publisher wants to license quotes from old episodes but needs timestamps and exact wording within three weeks. the raw audio lives on two external drives in inconsistent folder structures, some files named by date, others by guest. i edit everything myself in the evenings and my hosting plan caps uploads at 250 mb per file.

i am revising a 96,000 word fantasy novel before querying agents in september. three beta readers returned notes that contradict each other on pacing, and i have found at least a dozen continuity errors myself: a character's eye color changes, a city is two days' ride in one chapter and a week in another. my notes live in a mix of scrivener comments, a google doc, and margin scribbles in a printed copy. i write before my day job, roughly 90 minutes each morning, and i keep losing track of which fixes are actually done.

i manage the blog for a b2b saas company, around 460 published posts going back to 2018. organic traffic dropped 40 percent after the last search update and leadership wants it recovered by q4. many posts target the same keywords because three different writers never checked what already existed, and most tutorials show an old version of our product ui. i have one part-time writer, no developer time, and a cms with no way to review content in bulk. every post i touch needs sign-off from product marketing, which takes about a week per piece.

i run a woodworking youtube channel with 85,000 subscribers and upload one 15-minute video a week. retention graphs show 30 percent of viewers leaving in the first 45 seconds, before i even get to the build. each video starts as four to six hours of footage from two cameras and a phone, and i now spend more time editing than woodworking. my last five videos got fewer impressions than usual and i can't tell whether the thumbnails, the titles, or the topics are the problem. i film in my garage on weekends, so reshoots are impossible once a piece is glued up.

i am the only technical writer at a 60-person startup. product documentation is split between a public help center, a readme in the main repo, and a confluence space nobody has cleaned up since 2023. engineers ship changes that silently break the docs, and support only tells me after customers complain. last month a customer followed an outdated auth guide and locked themselves out for two days. i have no visibility into the release process, i learn about features at launch, and the help center search ranks archived pages above current ones.

i coordinate content for a travel booking site that publishes in english, german, spanish, french, and japanese. our freelance translators each keep their own glossary, so the same loyalty program has three different names in german depending on who picked up the job, and customers in japan keep asking about a feature name that doesn't match the app. turnaround commitments leave no room for extra review rounds, the translation budget is fixed for the year, and the source english copy changes weekly because the marketing team edits live pages without telling anyone.

i edit a daily email briefing for a city of 200,000 with a newsroom of three people. we receive around 70 press releases a day, most irrelevant, and the council uploads agenda packets as 300-page scanned pdfs the night before meetings. our best-read stories come from those packets, but reading one takes a full morning we rarely have. subscribers churn whenever the briefing skips a day, it has to go out by 6 a.m., and i'm usually writing it between 4:30 and 5:45 with whatever we managed to dig up.

i am the in-house photographer for a regional theater company. fifteen years of production photos, about 70,000 images, sit on a raid array organized only by show date. marketing asks for things like the shot of the red dress from any production of carmen, and finding it takes me an afternoon. none of the files carry keywords, performer names, or rights notes, and some older shoots have unclear usage permissions in the performers' contracts. we just lost a licensing sale because i couldn't confirm clearance in time. i can spare maybe three hours a week for the archive between shoots.

i write grants for a small environmental nonprofit, about 25 applications a year. every funder asks for roughly the same information in slightly different formats, and our boilerplate lives in a 90-page word doc that four people have edited with conflicting program numbers. last cycle i submitted an application containing the previous year's budget figures and we lost a 40,000 dollar renewal. deadlines cluster in march and october, program staff are slow to confirm current stats, and the development director wants every proposal to sound less templated even though i'm drawing from the same source text.

i run a 40,000 square foot warehouse that ships about 900 e-commerce orders a day. our fastest movers are slotted wherever space happened to be free when they arrived, so pickers crisscross the whole building and our pick rate has fallen to 55 lines per hour from 80 a year ago. receiving appointments cluster on mondays and thursdays, which jams the dock and pushes pallets into pick aisles. peak season starts in eight weeks, the landlord will not let us expand, and i can only absorb about six temps before training overwhelms my two supervisors.

i manage a fleet of 22 dry van trucks doing regional runs out of memphis. about a third of our return legs come back empty because our biggest customer ships outbound only, and diesel is eating the margin on those lanes. drivers bump against hours-of-service limits when receivers hold them at the dock for three or four hours, and detention is rarely paid. two of my best drivers quit last quarter over unpredictable home time. i have contracts i cannot drop until next year and a dispatcher who still plans everything on a whiteboard.

i own a wholesale bakery that supplies 60 cafes with bread and pastries six days a week. everything moves through two deck ovens, and laminated doughs need the cold room during the same hours the bread needs proofing space. orders arrive by text and voicemail until 10 pm and the night shift starts mixing at 11, so late changes mean either waste or shortages. on saturdays we throw away roughly 8 percent of production while two cafes complain about shorted croissants. my head baker keeps the schedule in his head and he wants to cut back to four nights.

i run an hvac service company with nine technicians covering a metro area about 50 miles across. dispatch assigns jobs first-come, so techs crisscross town and average four calls a day when the work could support six. each van carries a different mix of parts, and about a quarter of calls end in a second visit because the right capacitor or control board was not on the truck. summer surge doubles call volume, customers wait five days for non-emergency work, and our best installer spends half his day driving. the parts room has no inventory count i would trust.

i operate a 30-acre vegetable farm selling through a 250-member csa and two saturday markets. harvest crews pick to a plan i write on wednesday, but germination and weather swing actual yields 30 percent either way, so boxes get padded with whatever is abundant and members complain about kale fatigue. our only walk-in cooler is 8 by 12 feet, and everything harvested thursday has to fit alongside market stock. i lose a few hundred dollars of greens a week to wilting in the staging shed, and my two crew leads argue about pick order every single morning.

i manage a 14-person machine shop where one five-axis mill is the bottleneck for about 70 percent of our jobs. quoting promises lead times off a calendar nobody updates, so on-time delivery has slipped to 60 percent and our biggest aerospace customer is threatening to pull work. hot jobs get walked to the front of the queue by whoever shouts loudest, which means setups get torn down half-finished. setup on the mill takes two to five hours, we run a single shift, and i have no trustworthy picture of what the machine can actually produce in a week.

i coordinate home delivery for a furniture retailer running about 70 stops a day with eight two-person crews. roughly one in ten deliveries fails because the customer is not home, the sofa will not fit the stairwell, or the freight elevator was never reserved, and every failure costs a return trip plus warehouse re-handling. crews self-route from a printed manifest, so afternoon stops routinely miss their four-hour windows and complaints spike after 2 pm. damage claims run 3 percent of deliveries, mostly stairwell scuffs we cannot prove either way. on peak weekends we rent extra trucks but cannot find vetted second crew members.

i run distribution for a seafood wholesaler moving fresh product from two coastal suppliers to 90 restaurants within 24 hours of landing. our reefer vans hold temperature fine on the road, but product sits on the receiving dock during the morning sort and we have logged excursions above 40 degrees three times this month. when a chef rejects a box i cannot trace which lot it came from without digging through paper invoices for an afternoon. anything unsold by day two gets discounted to a fish market at a loss, and the health inspector wants temperature records we mostly do not have.

i am the site superintendent on a six-story infill building whose footprint covers the entire lot. there is no laydown yard, so every delivery has to be hoisted or carried in the day it arrives, and the single tower crane is booked solid from 7 to 3. drywall showed up two weeks early last month and blocked the electricians' staging area for days. subs schedule their own trucks, the street permit allows unloading only before 9 am, and neighbor noise complaints stop work while we respond. we are eleven days behind and the concrete pour dates cannot move.

i manage the yard for an equipment rental branch with about 400 assets, from skid steers to scissor lifts. returns pile up faster than my two mechanics can inspect them, so machines sit marked available in the system while actually awaiting checks, and counter staff promise units we cannot deliver. customers bring equipment back muddy, low on fuel, or damaged, and disputes drag on because check-in photos are inconsistent. saturday pickups all land between 7 and 9 am with one loader and one forklift driver on shift. utilization looks fine on paper while three of my big excavators have not turned in weeks.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i am trying to learn italian before a family trip in october. i have a 2,400-card anki deck i built over two years, but i imported three shared decks along the way and now there are near-duplicate cards with inconsistent formatting, audio on some and not others, and tags that mean nothing anymore. my retention stats look fine but i freeze in actual conversation. i can spare about 25 minutes a day on the train, with spotty cell coverage, and i do not want to lose my review history by starting over from scratch.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i have about 80,000 family photos spread across google photos, an old icloud account, and two external hard drives from previous laptops. many are duplicates at different resolutions, some exist as both edited and original versions, and the drives use a folder scheme i abandoned in 2019. my mom keeps asking for the pictures from my brother's wedding and i genuinely cannot find the full set. i pay for 2tb of google storage that is nearly full, my upload bandwidth is slow rural dsl, and i am afraid of deleting the only good copy of something.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i run a small home automation setup on a raspberry pi 4 in my closet: zigbee lights, a few temperature sensors, and automations i wrote in node-red two years ago. the sd card has corrupted twice this year, and each time i rebuild from memory because my only backup is a year-old disk image on my desktop. my partner is fed up because when the pi dies the bedroom lights stop responding to the wall switch entirely. i have maybe four hours a weekend for this and a growing suspicion that i no longer understand half the flows i wrote.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i am a freelance illustrator and my tax records are a mess. receipts arrive as paper, email pdfs, and photos on my phone, and i dump everything into a folder called sort later. last april i spent three full days reconstructing deductible expenses and i am sure i missed several hundred dollars worth. my accountant charges extra for disorganized records, quarterly estimated payments keep catching me off guard, and there is a stack of unopened envelopes from two banks and the irs on my kitchen counter that i am nervous to open.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i started teaching myself piano during lockdown and i have plateaued hard. i can play about six pieces from memorized finger patterns, but i still cannot read bass clef fluently and anything with syncopation falls apart. i practice 20 minutes most evenings on a 61-key keyboard in the living room, where i cannot make noise after my kid's 8pm bedtime. lessons near me cost 70 dollars an hour, which i cannot justify monthly. i keep restarting beginner books, getting bored at the parts i already know, and quitting before the parts i actually need.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i did a rough count and i am paying for at least 19 subscriptions across two credit cards, paypal, and my phone bill: streaming, cloud storage, a gym i visited twice this year, software i needed for one project, and a few charges i cannot identify from the statement descriptions at all. some renew annually so they only sting once and then i forget. my bank's app categorizes them inconsistently, a couple are billed in foreign currencies, and one cancellation requires a phone call during my own working hours. i estimate i am wasting 80 to 120 dollars a month but i do not actually know.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i cook most nights and my recipe collection is scattered across browser bookmarks, instagram saves, screenshots in my camera roll, a notes app, and a binder of my grandmother's handwritten cards. when i plan meals on sundays i can never find the thing i made three weeks ago that everyone liked. half my saved links now hit paywalls or deleted pages, the screenshots are unsearchable, and the binder lives at my parents' house. i shop for a family of five with one gluten-free kid, and i keep rebuying spices i already own because i never know what a week's plan actually requires.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i have been researching my family tree for three years and i have hit a wall of my own making. i have 312 people in an ancestry tree, a gedcom export from my late uncle's computer, scanned church records in polish that i cannot read, and a shoebox of unlabeled photographs from the 1940s. the two trees disagree on my great-grandmother's birth year and i no longer remember which sources support which date. my subscription renews at 240 dollars next month, the relatives who could identify the photos are in their late eighties, and my notes are split between two notebooks and an abandoned word doc.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i am moving to a new apartment across town in five weeks and the logistics keep multiplying. my current lease overlaps the new one by six days, which is also the only window the movers had available. i need to transfer electric and internet, but the new building only allows one specific isp with a three-week install backlog. my address is attached to roughly forty accounts, my car registration, my voter record, and a passport renewal currently in the mail to the old address. i work 9 to 6 with no flexibility and most offices i need to call keep the same hours i do.

problems.slice: personal projects, learning, and life admin
problems.text: i track my money in a google sheet i have maintained since 2021, with a tab per year and formulas i copy forward every month. in march my bank redesigned its csv export, the columns shifted, and my import broke in a way i did not notice for seven weeks, so my spending categories are silently wrong for almost two months. i share the sheet with my spouse, who only checks the summary tab and now distrusts the whole thing. i refuse to give a third-party app my bank credentials, i have about an hour a week for upkeep, and tax season showed me i cannot even trust my own totals.
